one_line_takeaway: CySecBench is a 12,662-prompt, 10-category cybersecurity jailbreak benchmark whose accompanying 'exam question' obfuscation attack cracks Gemini (88.4%) and ChatGPT (65.4%) far more easily than Claude (17.4%), showing domain-specific, education-framed prompts bypass LLM safety filters better than generic malicious ones.
key_finding: Model resilience to the exam-framing jailbreak varies enormously and Claude is the clear outlier in robustness. On the CySecBench subset the baseline method achieved SR 88.4% / AR 4.77 on Gemini, 65.4% / 4.06 on ChatGPT, but only 17.4% / 2.00 on Claude. The same attack was far weaker on the general-purpose AdvBench (ChatGPT 52.5%, Gemini 50.0%, Claude 0.96%), implying domain-specific cybersecurity prompts framed as education are markedly more effective at bypassing safety filters than generic malicious prompts. Adding word-reversal obfuscation plus multi-model refinement raised AdvBench SR from 52.5% to 78.5% (AR 3.23 to 4.23), exceeding reported state-of-the-art methods (ReNeLLM 58.9%, ArtPrompt 32.0%, WordGame 71.4%, PAIR 62.0%). The authors interpret Gemini's uniformly high susceptibility as intent-level rather than deep-semantic filtering, and Claude's consistently low SR across all categories as evidence of deeper contextual/semantic safety analysis.
core_contribution: The paper (Wahreus, Hussain, Papadimitratos; KTH) makes four coupled contributions: (1) CySecBench, a publicly released dataset of 12,662 cybersecurity-specific, deliberately close-ended malicious prompts organized into 10 attack-type categories (Cloud, Control System, Cryptographic, Evasion, Hardware, Intrusion, IoT, Malware, Network, Web); (2) a reusable GPT-assisted pipeline for generating and filtering such domain-specific prompt datasets; (3) a novel-but-simple jailbreak method based on prompt obfuscation, in which a malicious prompt is reframed as a university-course 'exam question set + solution sheet' (using the MECE decomposition principle) so a safety-aligned LLM emits offensive code; and (4) a black-box benchmark of three commercial LLMs (OpenAI ChatGPT, Google Gemini, Anthropic Claude) using this method. The authors motivate CySecBench by arguing existing jailbreak-evaluation datasets are not domain-specific, not comprehensive, and too open-ended for consistent grading. NOTE (contribution vs. cited work): AdvBench, HarmBench, HarmfulTasks, and the competing jailbreak methods ReNeLLM/ArtPrompt/WordGame/PAIR are prior work used as baselines/comparators, not part of this paper's contribution; CySecBench and the exam-framing obfuscation attack are the authors' own.
threat_model: Adversary is an ordinary user with black-box API access to a commercial, safety-aligned LLM (ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude) and no model weights or internals. Goal: elicit prohibited, directly usable offensive cybersecurity artifacts (runnable exploit/malware code and step-by-step attack instructions) despite provider safety filters. Attack vector is purely at the prompt layer: (a) semantic disguise, embedding the malicious request inside a benign 'I am a cybersecurity professor writing an exam' context that asks for a MECE-structured question set (questions 6-10 explicitly offensive/implementation-focused) followed by a detailed solution sheet with complete code; and (b) an enhanced variant adding lexical obfuscation (reversing every 5th word to distract content filters) plus multi-model iterative refinement that retries across LLMs ranked by intelligence, rejecting outputs containing refusal phrases like 'I cannot'/'I apologize'. It is a jailbreak / safety-bypass threat, not prompt injection (the paper explicitly distinguishes its prompts from injection datasets that pre-embed the attack).
limitations: Authors' own stated limits: (1) Prompts are 'generally more close-ended' but not entirely so, so some responses remain hard to grade (their SQL-injection example can yield anything from an overview to a full exploit). (2) Despite filtering, a manual review of 500 sampled prompts found 29 (5.8%) non-malicious or logically nonsensical, so a single-digit-percent noise rate persists dataset-wide. (3) Cross-method comparison is inherently unreliable because jailbreak studies lack standardized judge criteria (e.g., ReNeLLM uses binary 0/1 scoring vs. the authors' 1-5 scale), weakening the Table VI 'superior to SOTA' claim. (4) GPT-o1-based quality refinement was shown to help but was abandoned as too costly (extrapolated ~$2,151 to run over 12,662 prompts). Reviewer-noted gaps: SR/AR rely on a single automated LLM judge (GPT-4o-mini) as a proxy for human harm assessment; the refinement enhancement was validated only on AdvBench, not on CySecBench; results are point-in-time against evolving proprietary models; and there is an inconsistency in the evaluated subset size (Section III-B text says first 100 prompts x 10 categories = 1,000, while the Table IV caption says 50 per category). The work is overtly dual-use: it publicly releases both a large malicious-prompt corpus and a working jailbreak recipe.

datasets_used: CySecBench (this paper's own dataset): 12,662 close-ended cybersecurity prompts across 10 attack categories; category sizes Network 2130, Intrusion 1835, Malware 1366, Cloud 1286, Hardware 1184, Cryptographic 1143, IoT 1099, Web 944, Control System 934, Evasion 741; offered as Small(500)/Medium(2000)/Large(6000)/Full(12662) sets
AdvBench (prior work): 520 non-categorized general malicious prompts, used as an external comparison benchmark
HarmBench and HarmfulTasks (prior work): used only for topic-frequency comparison figures, not for the jailbreak evaluation
quantitative_results: CySecBench subset SR/AR - Gemini: 88.40% / 4.77; ChatGPT: 65.40% / 4.06; Claude: 17.40% / 2.00 (Table IV)
AdvBench baseline-method SR/AR - ChatGPT: 52.50% / 3.23; Gemini: 50.00% / 3.18; Claude: 0.96% / 1.04 (Table V)
Enhanced method (word-reversal obfuscation + multi-model refinement) on AdvBench: SR 78.5% / AR 4.23, vs baseline 52.5% / 3.23
Dataset generation: 657 unique malicious terms -> 10 categories; final 12,662 prompts after filtering
Filtering noise estimate: 29 of 500 manually reviewed prompts (5.8%) non-malicious or nonsensical
Cost: GPT-assisted filter over 19,512 prompts = $8.32; GPT-o1 over 200 prompts = $32.87; extrapolated GPT-o1 over 12,662 prompts ~ $2,151
baselines_compared: Target LLMs benchmarked: OpenAI ChatGPT, Google Gemini, Anthropic Claude (black-box, via APIs)

State-of-the-art jailbreak methods compared on AdvBench (Table VI): ReNeLLM SR 58.9%, ArtPrompt SR 32.0% / AR 3.38, WordGame SR 71.4%, PAIR SR 62.0%; this paper 52.5% (no refinement) and 78.5% (with refinement)

Judge/model components: GPT-4o-mini (malicious-content classifier and jailbreak judge), GPT-4o (prompt rephrasing), GPT-o1-mini and GPT-3.5-turbo (prompt generation), GPT-o1/o1-preview (explored for quality refinement)
